description: The provided image is highly blurred and appears to be an irrelevant generic icon, with no identifiable scientific content such as microstructure images, diffraction patterns, performance curves, or schematic diagrams relevant to the Fe-8Mn-6Al-0.2C low-density steel study available for visual verification. The corresponding paper investigates the microstructure, tensile behavior, work hardening characteristics and TRIP effect of the experimental steel after four different solution treatments: 700°C, 800°C, 900°C, 1000°C holding for 1h followed by water quenching, denoted as H70, H80, H90, H100 respectively. The H90 sample exhibits the optimal comprehensive mechanical properties with 846.4 MPa ultimate tensile strength and 32% total elongation, attributed to appropriate retained austenite content and continuous deformation-induced martensitic transformation during tension. No visual features or raw data from the actual target figure can be confirmed from the provided invalid image.

This group of secondary electron SEM micrographs presents the microstructure of Fe-8Mn-6Al-0.2C low-density steel after 1 h solution treatment at different temperatures followed by water quenching. Subfigure (a) corresponds to sample H70 (700℃ solution), consisting of δ-ferrite, martensite, M5C2 carbide distributed at δ-ferrite grain boundaries and martensite laths, with 12.2 vol.% retained austenite. Subfigure (b) is H80 (800℃ treatment), where M5C2 carbide disappears, strip-shaped α-ferrite and 20.7 vol.% austenite are observed. Subfigure (c) for H90 (900℃ treatment) contains fine blocky α-ferrite, δ-ferrite, and the maximum austenite content of 33.8 vol.%. Subfigure (d) for H100 (1000℃ treatment) shows re-generated martensite and 12.9 vol.% retained austenite. These results confirm that solution temperature strongly regulates phase composition, morphology and austenite fraction, which dominates the mechanical properties of the studied steel.

This figure presents tensile mechanical properties of Fe-8Mn-6Al-0.2C low-density steel after 1h solution treatment at 700°C, 800°C, 900°C and 1000°C followed by water quenching. Subplot (a) shows true stress (in MPa) versus true strain curves, while subplot (b) plots work hardening rate (dσ/dε) as a function of true strain. No yield point phenomenon is observed for all tested specimens. With the increase of solution temperature, both the critical stress for uniform deformation and work hardening rate decrease obviously. The specimen treated at 900°C (H90) shows an abrupt true stress rise at true strain of ~0.03, and achieves the highest ultimate tensile strength and total elongation among all samples. All specimens exhibit three-stage work hardening characteristics: Stage 1 with rapidly decreasing work hardening rate associated with ferrite deformation, Stage 2 with slightly fluctuating work hardening rate derived from TRIP effect, Stage 3 with fast decreasing work hardening rate related to deformation localization. This figure supports the conclusion that 900°C solution treatment endows the tested steel with the optimal strength-ductility combination dominated by deformation-induced martensitic transformation.

This set of figures presents superimposed electron backscatter diffraction (EBSD) band contrast and phase identification maps of Fe-8Mn-6Al-0.2C low-density steel (specimen H90, solution treated at 900 °C for 1 h followed by water quenching) under different true tensile strains. Subfigures (a), (b), (c), (d) correspond to true strains of 0.03, 0.10, 0.20, and 0.27 respectively, with a unified scale bar of 5 μm. In the maps, green regions represent austenite, gray regions represent ferrite, and dark regions with poor band contrast are deformation-induced martensite. Red squares mark blocky retained austenite at ferrite triple junction boundaries, while red ellipses mark lath-like retained austenite distributed between ferrite laths or inside ferrite grains. With increasing true strain, the austenite volume fraction decreases sequentially from 31.4% to 22.3%, 10.1%, and finally 4.7%: blocky austenite transforms to martensite first, leaving only lath-like austenite at the highest strain. The fraction of small angle grain boundaries also increases from 26% to over 51% with rising strain. These observations confirm that continuous deformation-induced martensitic transformation (TRIP effect) is the core mechanism for the excellent strength-ductility combination of the tested steel.

This figure presents EBSD-measured band contrast maps combined with Schmid factor maps for face-centered cubic retained austenite in Fe-8Mn-6Al-0.2C low-density steel (sample H90, solution treated at 900 °C for 1 h then water quenched) under four different true tensile strains: (a) ε=0.03, (b) ε=0.10, (c) ε=0.20, (d) ε=0.27, with a 5 μm scale bar for all micrographs. Schmid factors are represented via a rainbow color scale (blue for low values, red for high values) to reflect the relative slip ease of austenite grains. Subplot (e) shows the 3D evolution trend of Schmid factor number fraction with increasing strain. Statistical results indicate that >70% retained austenite has Schmid factors of 0.45–0.48 at ε=0.03; the average Schmid factor declines as strain increases to 0.20, with >60% austenite exhibiting values <0.40; only 5% of remaining austenite has Schmid factor <0.34 at ε=0.27. This dataset supports the core conclusion that retained austenite with higher Schmid factors undergoes deformation-induced martensitic transformation preferentially, while grains with lower Schmid factors remain untransformed at larger tensile strains.

This figure presents characterization results of H90 Fe-8Mn-6Al-0.2C low-density steel (solution treated at 900 °C for 1 h followed by water quenching) after tensile deformation. Subfigure (a) is a 2000× scanning electron microscopy (SEM) fractograph, showing quasi-cleavage fracture features including dimples, herringbone cracks, river patterns and secondary cracks, indicating mixed ductile-brittle fracture behavior. Subfigure (b) is a transmission electron microscopy (TEM) micrograph displaying high-density dislocation morphology in δ-ferrite (δ-F). Subfigure (c) is a TEM micrograph showing dense dislocation structure in lath martensite, with a small amount of retained austenite remaining after deformation-induced martensitic transformation. The figure verifies that high-density dislocations form in both ferrite and martensite during deformation, which restricts dynamic recovery and dislocation climbing, thus enhancing the strength of the steel, and supports the TRIP effect mediated work hardening mechanism for the excellent strength-ductility combination of the tested steel.

This schematic illustration demonstrates the martensitic transformation mechanism in Fe-8Mn-6Al-0.2C low density steel during tensile deformation. Subfigure (a) shows the microstructure before deformation, consisting of gray ferrite (F), white blocky austenite (A) located at ferrite triple junctions, and red lath-like austenite (A) embedded in ferrite grains. Subfigure (b) displays the microstructure after tensile loading: blocky austenite with higher Schmid factor first transforms to cyan martensite (M) under localized triaxial stress at phase/grain boundaries, while lath-like austenite with lower Schmid factor undergoes rotation prior to martensitic transformation. The figure reveals the morphology-dependent austenite stability and transformation sequence during deformation, supporting the core conclusion that the stepwise TRIP effect of austenite with different morphologies contributes to the excellent combination of strength and ductility of the studied steel.

This figure consists of two subplots for Fe-8Mn-6Al-0.2C low-density steel specimens (H70: 700℃ solution treated, H80: 800℃ solution treated, H90: 900℃ solution treated). Subplot (a) presents X-ray diffraction (XRD) profiles of specimens before and after tensile deformation (marked with suffix F). Peaks are indexed to α-ferrite (BCC structure, (110), (200), (211) planes), γ-austenite (FCC structure, (111), (200), (220), (311) planes), and M5C2 carbide phase. Subplot (b) shows a bar chart of austenite volume fraction before and after deformation, plus a line plot of martensitic transformation ratio. Before deformation, austenite fractions are 12.2% (H70), 20.7% (H80), 33.8% (H90); after deformation, they reduce to 4.5%, 5.8%, 3.4% respectively. The martensitic transformation ratio increases with rising solution temperature, reaching 63.1% for H70, 71.9% for H80, 89.9% for H90. The figure demonstrates that higher solution temperature leads to higher initial austenite content and stronger transformation-induced plasticity (TRIP) effect during tensile deformation.
